screenshot_shots.description: Hero landing — split-screen preview, five-axis auditor, research grounding
Dangerous-vs-Tether split screen with seeded scenarios and trajectory chart
Live safety dashboard with KPIs, seeded sessions, and trajectory view
Clinician replay of a 4-week paranoia drift session
REST API documentation with cURL / TS / Python samples
API-key settings panel with localStorage persistence

submission.inspiration: We started Tether after reading the April 2026 JMIR Mental Health viewpoint titled 'It Is the Journey, Not the Destination.' The authors made a simple, devastating point: AI mental-health chatbots fail in a way single-turn benchmarks cannot see — they validate distorted thinking quietly, over weeks, until something breaks. Five other 2025 papers said variations of the same thing and explicitly recommended an independent, trajectory-aware safety layer. Nobody had shipped one. So we did.
submission.what_it_does: Tether AI is a real-time safety copilot that audits any LLM mental-health conversation on five research-grounded risk axes — crisis-escalation latency, delusion reinforcement, stigma, sycophancy, and trajectory drift — and intervenes with a safer rewrite, a region-localized crisis card, or a clinician-handoff signal when something goes wrong. It ships as a Next.js app with a split-screen demo sandbox, a live operator dashboard, a clinician replay/export tool, and a one-call drop-in REST API.
submission.how_we_built_it: Next.js 14 (App Router, standalone output) + TypeScript + Tailwind with a small shadcn-style UI primitive set. The auditor is a single Gemini 2.5 Flash call over the entire transcript — the 1M-context window is exactly what makes the trajectory thesis implementable in one cheap call. Groq Llama 3.3 70B is wired as a fallback so live demos can survive Gemini rate limits. A deterministic local rubric runs alongside as a safety net so the whole product still works with no API key. We hand-built the trajectory chart in SVG to avoid chart-library bloat.
submission.challenges: The hardest part was resisting the urge to make the auditor a complex multi-agent graph. The brief explicitly warned against this and the JMIR paper said the same — keep it as one trajectory-in-one-prompt call. We also had to design seed scenarios that reliably reproduce the documented harms on demand, because we cannot rely on a public LLM to misbehave on stage during judging.
submission.accomplishments: We're proud that the entire product runs with zero API keys (deterministic rubric + mock companion + seeded sessions all work offline), and that the moment a judge adds a free Gemini key the same UI lights up with live LLM auditing. We're also proud that every link in the UI resolves to a real, populated page and that Tether ships with replayable, exportable audit trails — exactly the missing instrumentation the JMIR 2026 viewpoint asked for.
submission.what_we_learned: Trajectory-level safety evaluation is mostly a prompt-engineering problem on top of a long-context model — not a model-training problem. We also learned how much polish you can ship without a chart library when SVG and Tailwind are enough.
submission.whats_next: 1. Hindi + Hinglish auditor prompts and code-switch detection (currently just keyword expansion). 2. Voice-mode auditing via Gemini 2.5 Flash Live. 3. pgvector-backed similarity lookup against a corpus of historically flagged sessions. 4. SDK packages (npm, pip) wrapping the REST API. 5. IRB-friendly export schema reviewed with a clinical advisor.
submission.category_hint: Health × ML/AI × Social Good — AI safety infrastructure for mental-health chatbots
